name: Ammit (devourer)
shortDescription: Ammit was a female demon in ancient Egyptian religion with the head of a crocodile, the body of a lion, and the hindquarters of a hippopotamus. She devoured the souls deemed unworthy in the afterlife.
description: Ammit, also known as Ammut or Ahemait, was a fearsome demoness in ancient Egyptian mythology known as the 'Devourer of the Dead'. Represented with the head of a crocodile, the forequarters of a lion or leopard, and the hindquarters of a hippopotamus, she embodied the three largest 'man-eating' animals known to the ancient Egyptians, making her a symbol of immense danger. Her role was crucial in the weighing of the heart ceremony in Duat (the underworld). After Anubis weighed the heart of the deceased against the feather of Ma'at (truth and justice), Thoth recorded the results. If the heart was heavier than the feather, indicating a life filled with sin and imbalance, Ammit would devour the heart, condemning the soul to a state of restless oblivion, effectively preventing them from achieving eternal life. She was not worshipped but feared, representing the ultimate punishment for a life lived without regard for Ma'at. Ammit served as a constant reminder of the importance of living a righteous life, ensuring cosmic balance, and adhering to the principles of truth and justice.
appearance: Head of a crocodile, forequarters of a lion or leopard, hindquarters of a hippopotamus.

sources.title: Dictionary of Ancient Deities
sources.author: Patricia Turner and Charles Russell Coulter
